protocol: environmental - heat exposure/heat exhaustion environmental - heat stroke (protocol 5 - 2)

dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 13 responding for a reported seizure at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, my name is Heidi, I'm one of the EMS providers here today. We were called for a possible seizure or stroke. What's your name?
Her name is Alice. We believe she had a seizure about an hour ago.
Tell me what you saw during the seizure, okay?
We heard her call out. She began to shake, mostly on her left side. Then she stopped moving for about a second, but then started moving again.
Alice, can you listen to me? Can you point to your nose if you're awake?
Okay.
Great, you opened your eyes to voice. Can you tell me your name?
Alice, yes.
Okay. Now if you can follow my finger, try to move your limbs for me, starting with your right arm.
Hmm, yes.
Your airway looks patent. Are you having any trouble breathing?
No, I feel fine now.
Alice, we need to check your breathing now. We're going to clip a device onto your finger. Can you hold it steady?
SpO₂ is 100%. Looks great.
Breathing appears even and regular. Chest rise and fall looks symmetrical. Let me listen to your lungs for a moment.
Let me check your pulse. Your pulse is 79 and your peripheral pulses are strong.
Blood pressure is 169/103. Skin is warm, pink, and dry. Good signs.
How would you rate the pain you had during the seizure, on a scale from 0 to 10?
10, but it went down after it finished.
Can you tell me more about what happened today?
Sure. She started with slurred speech around 1:00 PM. Then her left arm went weak and wouldn't move. She took a couple of minutes to recover.
I could tell something was wrong. My left side felt all weak and body twitching started.
We've heard from your family that this could be related to a medical history. Tell me, what do you know about your medical records?
I have had a stroke before. Actually, two strokes. I also have epilepsy for many years. I take a lot of medications to manage that and to manage my heart.
Can you tell me your age and estimated weight?
I'm 61 years old and I weigh around 185 pounds.
We noticed some twitching in your face on the left side. That's a new symptom for today, isn't it?
Yes, that's new. I can't even smile on the left side now.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Heidi
dialogue.utterance: Your nose and mouth look clear. Are you having any troubles with speech?

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Patient
dialogue.utterance: My speech is slowly coming back. I still feel a little slurred, but it's not too bad.
Alice, we are going to place you on a heart monitor. You'll feel a couple of electrode pads attached to your chest.
Going on now with a four-lead monitor. We just have leads on your limbs.
Monitoring shows a normal sinus rhythm for now. No acute changes.
We will do a 12-lead ECG as well for more detailed analysis. Hold still for a moment.
12-lead ECG in progress. Shows subtle axis deviations and minimal evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy, with ST and T wave abnormalities. These suggest possible lateral ischemia.
Left arm is still showing a bit of twitching and drift. We're going to check your pulse in both arms to compare.
Both pulses strong and equal. No broken skin or deformities. Left arm movements limited.
Let's check blood sugar. You may feel a small prick here on the finger. Ready?
Blood glucose is zero at this time. That's a concern-we'll need to keep rechecking.
Pulse now checks in at 82.
Respirations are now 25. That's slightly up from 23.
Blood pressure is now 187/108. It's continuing to trend upward.
SpO₂ is 99% and stable.
Let's re-monitor now. Rhythm shows sinus rhythm with subtle axis deviations, minimal evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy, and ST/T wave abnormalities.
GCS is 15 now. Your responses are complete and coherent.
Time to place an IV in your left forearm, Alice. We'll be using an 18-gauge needle in an extremity vein. That's a standard small-diameter line for fluids or medications if needed.
Needle placed. IV secure. Ready to use if we need fluids.
Pulse has increased to 96 this time.
Respirations still 23. Still no sign of distress.
Blood pressure has reached 233/123. That's a significant rise.
SpO₂ remains stable at 99%.
Blood glucose is now 101. We are beginning to see recovery with glucose levels.
IV infusing smoothly and monitoring is still in place. No medications administered yet.
What are your current pain levels or any new symptoms?
Still some discomfort in my left side, but manageable. No new symptoms.
Is your speech back to normal or is it still slurred?
It's getting better each minute. I still feel it, but it's not overwhelming.
Given the neurological and EKG findings, we are following protocol for stroke assessment along with protocol 5-2 for environmental heat conditions.
Based on your medical history and current readings, we will be transporting you to [COMPANY_NAME].
Time to place you onto the stretcher now. Secure harness is on.
Everything is ready. We'll be taking off soon.
Beginning handoff to the ED. Let me confirm the medications you're currently taking.
She's on gabapentin, Apixaban, atorvastatin, levetiracetam, and a few other medications to manage heart and seizure conditions.

We've taken recordings showing subtle axis deviations, minimal evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy, and ST and T wave abnormalities in the 12-lead ECG. These support a possible cardiac etiology. We also noted suspected CVA (stroke) with neurological findings, such as left-side weakness and slurred speech.
I got that. We will manage the same at the ED. Thank you for the thorough report.
All right, beginning our transport to the hospital.
Ambulance moving. Patient is buckled on the stretcher with all monitoring active.
All patient consents were not obtained. Everything set and ready for arrival.
